research_gaps: Exact genesis token distribution percentages vary across sources -- whitepaper figures (29% community, 26% team) differ from some secondary reports
Provider count (63 active) may fluctuate significantly; no historical provider count data publicly available
BME (Burn-Mint Equilibrium) code is complete but activation date remains 'early-mid 2026' -- no firm governance proposal yet
Chain deprecation timeline ('late 2026') is aspirational with no published technical roadmap or migration specification
Destination chain for migration not confirmed -- Solana described as 'strong contender' but no governance vote
No formal protocol-level security audit disclosed despite operating since 2020
Starbonds ($75M regulated securities offering) terms and investor details not yet public
Exact GPU utilisation methodology not disclosed -- 60% figure is self-reported
Annual revenue ($3.15M) source methodology unclear -- may be annualised from shorter periods
Homenode (beta Feb 2026) metrics and adoption numbers not yet available
Number of unique tenants/deployers not publicly tracked or reported
Greg Osuri's personal AKT holdings not publicly disclosed

identity.name: Akash Network
identity.one_liner: A permissionless cloud computing marketplace where providers list spare capacity and tenants deploy containers via reverse auction, at a fraction of centralised cloud costs.
identity.chain: Cosmos (Akash appchain, Cosmos SDK v0.53 + CometBFT)

founding_team.name: Greg Osuri
founding_team.background: Serial open-source entrepreneur. Created AnyCast (acquired by Cloudflare), co-created Kubernetes Helm package manager. Early contributor to Cosmos SDK. Over 10,000 GitHub contributions. Based in the US.

founding_team.name: Adam Bozanich
founding_team.background: Software engineer with background in distributed systems. Joined Greg Osuri in founding Overclock Labs. Leads protocol and infrastructure development.

rounds.notes: Early private round details sparse. CoinGecko lists Cypher Capital, Nika Osuri, Hiro Systems, Cosmostation as investors.
IEO on BitMax at $0.39 per AKT. Small raise by crypto standards.

technical.architecture_summary: Akash is a Cosmos SDK appchain (v0.53 + CometBFT) running a decentralised cloud marketplace. Tenants describe workloads in SDL (Stack Definition Language, YAML-based), submit deployment orders on-chain, and providers bid via reverse auction. The lowest qualifying bid wins. Workloads run in Kubernetes containers on provider infrastructure. The chain handles order matching, escrow payments, and provider attestation. Compute execution happens off-chain on provider hardware. GPU marketplace launched September 2023 (NVIDIA only). AkashML managed inference service launched for simplified AI model deployment.
technical.consensus_mechanism: Delegated Proof of Stake (DPoS) via CometBFT (formerly Tendermint). 100 active validators. ~6 second block time.
technical.what_is_onchain: Deployment orders, provider bids, reverse auction matching, escrow payments, staking/delegation, governance proposals, provider attestation, certificate management
technical.what_is_offchain: Actual compute workload execution (Kubernetes containers), provider infrastructure, storage, networking, model inference (AkashML)

security_incidents.description: Critical authentication bypass vulnerability in provider services discovered by ChainLight (formerly Theori). Allowed potential unauthorised access to provider infrastructure. Patched promptly via coordinated disclosure. No funds lost, no exploitation in the wild confirmed.
Spam attack on the network caused degraded performance. Overclock Labs responded with network parameter adjustments. Details sparse but referenced in community discussions.

token_utility: Staking to validators (secure the network, earn inflation rewards)
Provider incentives (earn AKT for serving workloads)
Take fee revenue (network collects a percentage of deployment spend)

initial_allocation.notes: Community allocation for ecosystem growth and adoption
Standard vesting schedule for founding team and early advisors
Reserved for long-term network development and operations
Genesis allocation to bootstrap validator set. Ongoing inflation generates additional AKT.

distribution.distribution_method: IEO on BitMax (AscendEX) at $0.39 per AKT (October 2020). Genesis supply of 100M AKT. Ongoing inflation distributes new tokens to stakers. Not a fair launch -- team and investors received allocations, but raise was small ($2.8M) and team vesting was 4 years.

emissions.halving_schedule: No halving. Inflation rate adjustable via governance (currently 8-13% range depending on staking ratio). Cosmos SDK adjusts inflation to target a specific bonding ratio.
emissions.burn_mechanism: BME (Burn-Mint Equilibrium): code complete, not yet live. When activated, AKT spent on compute is burned and new AKT minted to providers, creating deflationary pressure proportional to network usage. Currently, deployment payments flow through escrow without burning.

staking.staking_type: Delegated Proof of Stake (nominator staking via Cosmos SDK)
staking_risks: Inflation erodes value if network usage doesn't grow (real yield near zero when adjusted for inflation)
21-day unbonding locks capital during volatile periods
Slashing risk for double-signing (5%) or prolonged downtime (0.01%)
Validator selection risk -- poorly-run validators can be slashed
Chain migration risk -- staking mechanics may change with new chain
staking.slashing_conditions: Double-signing: 5% slash. Extended downtime (missing blocks): 0.01% slash and jailing.

tokenomics.token_model_classification: Inflationary utility token with planned deflationary mechanism (BME)
tokenomics.tokenomics_notes: AKT currently operates as a standard Cosmos inflationary staking token. The real tokenomics thesis depends on BME activation: when compute spend is significant enough to burn more AKT than inflation creates, the token becomes deflationary. At $3.15M annual revenue and ~$85M market cap, BME alone cannot drive meaningful deflation yet. The gap between the tokenomics vision (revenue-driven burns) and current reality (inflation-dependent staking) is the core investment question. NOT listed on Binance spot, which limits liquidity and discovery. ~96% below ATH.

methods.description: Delegate AKT to validators on the Akash chain. Earn inflation rewards (~10% APY nominal). Vote on governance proposals through your validator or directly.
methods.estimated_returns: ~10% APY nominal (near zero real yield after inflation)

methods.description: Run a cloud provider node on Akash. List spare compute (CPU, GPU, memory, storage) on the marketplace. Earn AKT from tenant deployments. Permissionless -- anyone with qualifying hardware can join.
methods.minimum_hardware: Server-grade hardware with Kubernetes capability. GPU providers need NVIDIA cards (consumer or data centre). Reliable internet, static IP preferred.
methods.estimated_returns: Variable -- depends on hardware listed, utilisation rate, and bid competitiveness. GPU providers currently earning more than CPU-only.

methods.description: Deploy containerised workloads on Akash as a tenant. Use SDL (YAML-based) to describe requirements, submit to marketplace, providers bid. Typically 50-85% cheaper than AWS/GCP/Azure for comparable compute.
methods.minimum_hardware: None (client-side CLI or web console only)
methods.estimated_returns: Cost savings vs centralised cloud. Not a yield-generating activity.
barriers_to_entry: No managed services (databases, load balancers) -- raw compute only

methods.description: Build on or integrate with Akash. Four funding mechanisms available: Community Pool (on-chain treasury), Akash Accelerate (grants programme), Akash Bounties Board, and AkashML partnerships.
methods.estimated_returns: Grant funding available. No direct token emissions to builders.
barriers_to_entry: Must propose to community pool via governance or apply to grants programme

developer_resources.developer_community_size: 350+ GitHub contributors across 65 repos. Core node repo has 34 contributors.

usage_metrics.compute_delivered: 63 active providers, ~700 GPUs (NVIDIA), CPU/memory/storage also available. ~60% GPU utilisation (self-reported). AkashML managed inference service operational.
usage_metrics.revenue_source: Take fee on deployment spend (percentage of tenant payments to providers). $3.15M annual revenue in 2025 (up 128% year-over-year).
usage_metrics.metrics_source: Akash Network Stats, Overclock Labs reporting
usage_metrics.usage_notes: Key customers include Venice.ai (GPU inference), ElizaOS (AI agent framework), and Morpheus (decentralised AI). Gensyn integration for distributed training. 63 providers is a small but growing number -- up from single digits at GPU launch in 2023. Revenue of $3.15M against ~$85M market cap gives a price-to-revenue ratio of ~27x, which is reasonable for growth-stage infrastructure. GPU utilisation at 60% suggests genuine demand but the absolute base (700 GPUs) is tiny compared to centralised alternatives (AWS has millions of GPUs).

community.governance_participation_rate: Active -- multiple governance proposals passed including inflation adjustments and community pool spending
community.governance_model: Cosmos SDK on-chain governance. Token-weighted voting with delegation. Any AKT holder can submit proposals (with deposit). Validators vote on behalf of delegators by default; delegators can override. Standard Cosmos governance parameters.
key_community_channels: Akash Network Stats (stats.akash.network) -- live marketplace data
community.sentiment_summary: Community is technically oriented and practically focused. Strong conviction around the 'decentralised AWS' thesis. GPU marketplace launch re-energised interest after years of CPU-only offering. Key concerns: 96% price decline from ATH, chain migration uncertainty, small provider count. Venice.ai's adoption as a customer is a frequently cited validation. Community frustrated by lack of Binance listing and slow BME activation.
community.community_notes: Greg Osuri is highly visible on X and at conferences. His open-source background (Kubernetes Helm, AnyCast/Cloudflare) gives credibility. Community culture is more DevOps/infrastructure-oriented than typical crypto communities. Akash Insiders programme and Akash Accelerator provide structured contributor pathways.

infrastructure_decentralisation.evidence: 63 active providers with permissionless participation -- anyone with qualifying hardware can join. 100 DPoS validators run the chain. ~700 GPUs deployed across independent providers. Provider hardware is geographically distributed. However: 63 providers is objectively thin for a 'cloud marketplace'. Provider concentration means a handful of large providers serve the majority of capacity. Planned chain deprecation (late 2026) introduces major uncertainty -- migration to a new chain (Solana mentioned as candidate) means current infrastructure decentralisation could be rebuilt from scratch. Overclock Labs remains the dominant development entity.

governance_decentralisation.evidence: On-chain Cosmos SDK governance with token-weighted voting. Proposals have been submitted and passed by the community, including inflation adjustments and community pool spending. Validators vote on behalf of delegators with override capability. However: Overclock Labs drives the development roadmap and strategic direction. Chain deprecation and Starbonds ($75M regulated securities offering) appear to be company decisions, not community-governed. Foundation/team hold significant voting power through token allocations. Governance is functional but Overclock Labs retains outsized influence.

token_distribution_fairness.evidence: IEO at $0.39 with public participation (Oct 2020). Genesis 100M AKT. Team allocation ~26% with 4-year vesting and 1-year cliff -- standard and reasonable. Community allocation ~29%. Total raise of $2.8M is notably small, meaning limited VC capture. Ongoing inflation distributes new tokens to stakers. Not a fair launch, but the IEO model with small raise and meaningful vesting is above average for the era.

censorship_resistance.evidence: Permissionless provider onboarding -- anyone can list compute. Tenants deploy arbitrary containers with no protocol-level content filtering. Reverse auction bidding is neutral. Providers can individually choose what to host, creating a natural market rather than centralised censorship. DPoS validators do not inspect or filter individual deployments. However: Overclock Labs as a US-incorporated entity (Delaware) is subject to regulatory pressure. If compelled, they could propose governance changes or coordinate validator responses. The small provider count (63) means coordinated pressure on providers is more feasible than in a larger network.

data_sovereignty.evidence: Tenants fully control their deployment configurations and data. Self-custodial Cosmos wallets (Keplr). No platform-level data collection or surveillance. Deployments run in isolated Kubernetes containers. However: providers physically host tenant workloads and could theoretically inspect container contents (though this would violate trust assumptions). No encryption at the compute layer. No zero-knowledge or confidential computing. Data in transit between tenant and provider is not end-to-end encrypted at the protocol level.

open_source_transparency.evidence: Fully open source under Apache 2.0 across 65 repositories. Active development with frequent commits. Cosmos SDK provides a battle-tested and well-audited framework. On-chain governance is transparent and verifiable. Revenue and provider metrics are publicly accessible. However: no formal protocol-level security audit has been disclosed despite operating since 2020. The ChainLight vulnerability (May 2024) was found through third-party research, not a commissioned audit. Overclock Labs' corporate financials are not public. Starbonds offering introduces a new opaque financial dimension.

freedom_score.freedom_summary: Akash scores a C (63/100), reflecting genuine infrastructure decentralisation with meaningful but incomplete governance distribution. The permissionless provider model and Cosmos-based on-chain governance are structurally sound -- meaningfully more decentralised than centralised cloud alternatives and more transparent than many crypto projects. However, 63 active providers is thin for a cloud marketplace, Overclock Labs retains outsized influence over strategic direction, and the planned chain deprecation introduces significant uncertainty. No formal security audit for a 5+ year old protocol is a notable gap. If Akash successfully executes its chain migration and activates BME with growing provider diversity, the freedom score would improve materially.

what_works_today: Working cloud marketplace with real deployments, real revenue ($3.15M/year), and real customers (Venice.ai, ElizaOS, Morpheus)
Permissionless provider model -- anyone with hardware can participate and earn
50-85% cheaper than centralised cloud for comparable compute
GPU marketplace (since Sep 2023) addresses the AI compute demand wave
Cosmos-based on-chain governance with active community participation
Fully open source (Apache 2.0) with strong developer credibility (Greg Osuri's Kubernetes/Cloudflare background)
AkashML managed inference simplifies AI workload deployment
Listed on Coinbase, Kraken, KuCoin -- decent exchange coverage
what_is_hype: 'Decentralised AWS' narrative is aspirational -- 63 providers with 700 GPUs is not comparable to AWS's scale
60% GPU utilisation sounds strong but the absolute base is tiny (~700 GPUs vs millions at hyperscalers)
Chain migration framed as 'evolution' but is effectively rebuilding the entire infrastructure layer
Revenue growth (128% YoY) is from a very small base ($1.4M to $3.15M) -- not yet meaningful at infrastructure scale
Starbonds ($75M securities offering) is a corporate fundraise dressed up as community-aligned infrastructure
BME deflationary narrative is premature -- current revenue cannot drive meaningful burns relative to inflation
key_risks: Chain deprecation (late 2026) is the single highest-impact risk -- migrating an entire blockchain and marketplace is technically complex and could fracture the community
63 active providers is not meaningfully decentralised for a cloud marketplace -- provider concentration risk is real
96% below all-time high ($0.30 vs $8.07) -- sustained price decline undermines provider incentives and community morale
No formal protocol audit despite 5+ years of operation -- ChainLight auth bypass vulnerability (May 2024) demonstrates real attack surface
Overclock Labs is a US-incorporated entity (Delaware) -- regulatory exposure to SEC, CFTC, and compute export controls
Greg Osuri key person risk -- he is the public face, strategic driver, and open-source credibility anchor
BME activation delay -- the core deflationary mechanism remains unactivated after years of development
Not listed on Binance -- limits Asian market liquidity and retail discovery
Spam attack (March 2025) demonstrated network resilience gaps
Starbonds ($75M) introduces regulatory and dilution risk -- terms not yet public
competitive_advantages: Longest-running decentralised cloud marketplace -- operating since 2020 with real workloads
Greg Osuri's open-source credibility (AnyCast/Cloudflare, Kubernetes Helm) is genuine and verifiable
Cost advantage (50-85% cheaper) addresses a real pain point for compute buyers
Cosmos SDK provides battle-tested infrastructure with IBC interoperability
Small funding raise ($2.8M) means less VC overhang and misaligned incentives than heavily-funded competitors
AI/GPU demand wave aligns perfectly with core offering
competitive_disadvantages: 63 providers vs thousands of data centres at AWS/GCP/Azure -- scale gap is enormous
No managed services (databases, load balancers, CDN) -- raw compute only, limiting enterprise adoption
Chain migration will reset much of the technical momentum built on Cosmos
Cosmos ecosystem is shrinking in mindshare relative to Ethereum L2s and Solana
No Binance listing limits liquidity compared to Render (RNDR), Bittensor (TAO), and others
Raw compute is a commodity -- differentiation relies on cost and decentralisation, both of which larger competitors can eventually match
comparable_projects: Render Network (RNDR) -- decentralised GPU rendering and compute
Bittensor (TAO) -- decentralised AI network (different model but competes for AI compute narrative)
assessment.outlook: Akash occupies a unique position as the longest-running decentralised cloud marketplace with real revenue and recognisable customers. The GPU marketplace launch (2023) positioned it well for the AI compute wave. The next 12-18 months are make-or-break: successful chain migration, BME activation, and continued provider growth would validate the thesis. Failure on any of these fronts -- especially chain migration -- could permanently damage momentum. The $75M Starbonds raise suggests Overclock Labs is betting on aggressive expansion, but the regulatory and execution risks are significant. For practitioners: AKT staking offers yield but real returns after inflation are near zero. Providing compute on Akash is the more interesting participation mode if you have idle hardware. The investment thesis depends on believing that decentralised cloud can capture meaningful market share from hyperscalers -- a bet on a structural shift, not short-term catalysts.
assessment.overall_assessment: Akash is the most credible decentralised cloud marketplace operating today, with real revenue, real customers, and a founder whose open-source credentials are independently verifiable. However, the gap between narrative ('decentralised AWS') and reality (63 providers, 700 GPUs, $3.15M revenue) is substantial. The planned chain deprecation is a high-risk strategic bet that could either accelerate growth (if the new chain is better) or fracture the project (if execution falters). No formal security audit after 5 years is indefensible for production infrastructure. For the OYM thesis: Akash demonstrates that decentralised compute can work at a functional level, but it has not yet proven it can work at meaningful scale. Worth tracking closely, but the chain migration must succeed before confidence increases.
